hints: A property describing opposition to current flow in a circuit
Extends the idea of resistance to AC circuits as a complex number
Combines resistance and reactance into one value
Symbol Z, measured in ohms
The key thing you match between speakers and amplifiers for maximum power transfer
A passive component found in nearly every circuit
Stores energy in an electric field between two conducting plates
Blocks DC while allowing AC to pass through
Measured in farads (F) — most practical capacitors are in microfarads or picofarads
Common types include electrolytic, ceramic, and film capacitors
The most fundamental relationship in basic circuit analysis
Named after German physicist Georg Simon Ohm, published in 1827
Describes how voltage, current, and resistance relate in a linear circuit
Double the resistance, halve the current — all else equal
Formula: V = IR, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance
The fundamental building block of modern electronics
Invented at Bell Labs in 1947 by Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley
Acts as either an amplifier or a switch depending on the circuit
Billions of them are packed into every modern CPU
Two main types: bipolar junction (BJT) and field-effect (MOSFET)
A passive component that stores energy in a magnetic field
Made from a coil of wire, sometimes wound around a ferromagnetic core
Opposes changes in current — if current tries to change, it pushes back
Measured in henries (H)
Used in filters, transformers, and switching power supplies; its opposition to AC is called inductive reactance
A two-terminal component that allows current to flow in only one direction
Used in power supplies to convert AC to DC
Has an anode (+) and a cathode (−); current flows from anode to cathode
A forward voltage drop of roughly 0.7 V is typical for silicon types
The LED (light-emitting diode) is a special version that emits light when forward-biased
The electrical 'pressure' that drives current through a circuit
Measured in volts (V), named after Alessandro Volta
Always measured between two points — it's a potential difference
A 9 V battery maintains this value between its terminals
Formally defined as energy per unit charge: V = W/Q, in joules per coulomb
The simplest passive component — it opposes current flow
Found in virtually every circuit to set voltages and limit currents
Colour bands on its body encode its resistance value
Dissipates electrical energy as heat
Measured in ohms (Ω); power rating (watts) determines how much heat it can safely handle
A high-gain differential amplifier in a small integrated circuit package
The workhorse of analog circuit design
Ideally has infinite input impedance and zero output impedance
Used to build amplifiers, filters, comparators, and oscillators
The LM741 and LM358 are classic examples; with negative feedback it becomes extremely versatile
A technique for controlling power delivery using rapidly switching signals
Used to dim LEDs, control motor speed, and regulate voltages
The output is always either fully on or fully off — never in between
The ratio of on-time to total period is called the duty cycle
Stands for Pulse Width Modulation — a 50% duty cycle means half on, half off
Two fundamental rules for analyzing electrical circuits
Named after German physicist Gustav Kirchhoff, formulated in 1845
The current law (KCL): current into a node equals current out
The voltage law (KVL): voltages around any closed loop sum to zero
Together with Ohm's Law, they let you solve any linear circuit systematically
A device that transfers electrical energy between circuits via electromagnetic induction
The reason AC power can be transmitted efficiently over long distances
Steps voltage up or down depending on the ratio of primary to secondary windings
No moving parts — operates entirely through magnetic coupling
Formula: V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂, where N is the number of turns in each winding
The reference point from which voltages in a circuit are measured
Not always connected to the physical earth — it's just the zero-volt reference
Every circuit needs one — without a reference, voltage measurements are meaningless
Shown on schematics as a downward triangle or horizontal lines
Earth ground in mains wiring is a safety feature; signal ground is a reference — confusing them causes problems
The number of complete cycles per second of a periodic signal
Mains electricity runs at 50 Hz (most of the world) or 60 Hz (Americas/Japan)
Measured in hertz (Hz), named after Heinrich Hertz
Higher frequencies carry more data — why 5G uses higher bands than 4G
Inverse of period: f = 1/T, where T is the time for one full cycle
An unintended low-resistance path between two points in a circuit
Causes excessive current to flow, often destroying components
What happens when a wire accidentally bridges two terminals of a battery
Fuses and circuit breakers exist specifically to protect against this
In an ideal short: resistance = 0 Ω, so by Ohm's Law, current → ∞
A component that converts real-world analog signals into digital numbers
Found in every microcontroller, smartphone, and audio interface
Resolution — typically 8, 10, or 12 bits — determines how finely it can distinguish levels
A microphone's output passes through one before a processor can work with it
Stands for Analog-to-Digital Converter; the reverse process is done by a DAC
A measure of how efficiently electrical power is being used
Ranges from 0 to 1 — a value of 1 means all power drawn does useful work
Caused by reactive components (inductors and capacitors) that store and return energy
Industrial facilities pay penalties for a low value because it wastes grid capacity
Defined as the cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current: PF = cos(φ)
The most essential test instrument in electronics
Displays voltage as a function of time on a screen
Used to debug signals, measure frequency, and spot noise or glitches
Probes connect to a circuit; trigger settings determine when the display locks onto a repeating signal
Modern versions are digital (DSO) and can store, analyze, and export waveforms
The frequency at which an LC circuit naturally oscillates
At this frequency, inductive and capacitive reactances are equal and cancel each other
The basis of radio tuning — adjusting a capacitor shifts this to select a station
Also appears in mechanical systems — the Tacoma Narrows Bridge had one that wind excited
Formula: f₀ = 1 / (2π√(LC)), where L is inductance and C is capacitance
An electrically operated switch
Allows a low-power signal to control a high-power circuit with no direct connection
Used in cars, industrial equipment, and home appliances
Contains a coil that generates a magnetic field to mechanically move a contact
Solid-state relays (SSRs) do the same job with no moving parts, using transistors instead
A measure of how much useful signal there is relative to background noise
Higher is better — a high value means a clean, strong signal
Critical in audio, radio, and sensor design
Measured in decibels (dB)
Defined as 10·log₁₀(P_signal / P_noise) for power, or 20·log₁₀(V_signal / V_noise) for voltage
A circuit that converts AC into DC
The first stage inside every power adapter and charger
A half-wave version uses one diode; a full-wave bridge uses four
The output is pulsating DC — a smoothing capacitor is added to flatten it
Without one, your laptop or phone couldn't use mains electricity
In electronics, the range of frequencies a circuit or component can handle
An audio amplifier's bandwidth determines which frequencies it reproduces faithfully
Measured in hertz (Hz) or megahertz (MHz)
Defined as the frequency range over which gain stays within 3 dB of its peak
Wider bandwidth means faster signal processing — critical in RF and data communications
A small computer on a single integrated circuit
Contains a processor, memory, and programmable I/O — all in one chip
Arduino boards are built around one, making them popular for prototyping
Used in appliances, toys, cars, and industrial equipment
Different from a CPU — it's designed for specific embedded control tasks, not general computing
A one-time overcurrent protection device
Contains a thin wire that melts and breaks the circuit when current exceeds its rating
Found in cars, power supplies, and mains plugs
Rated in amperes — a 5 A fuse blows when current exceeds 5 A
Unlike a circuit breaker, it cannot be reset — it must be replaced after it blows
The energy per unit charge supplied by a source to drive current around a circuit
Informally called 'voltage' of a battery or generator, though technically distinct
A battery's rated voltage is this value under open-circuit (no load) conditions
Under load, terminal voltage drops below it due to internal resistance
Stands for Electromotive Force — measured in volts
The most common electrical test instrument
Measures voltage, current, and resistance in a single handheld device
Most modern versions are digital (DMM) with an LCD readout
A continuity beep tells you a circuit is unbroken without having to read the display
Misusing it — e.g. measuring current without the correct probe jack — can blow its internal fuse
The ratio of output to input in an amplifier
A gain of 10 means the output is 10 times larger than the input
Can be expressed as a plain ratio or in decibels (dB)
Too much of it without negative feedback causes instability and oscillation
In dB: Gain (dB) = 20·log₁₀(V_out / V_in) for voltage
The flat board that mechanically supports and electrically connects electronic components
The green (or sometimes blue/red/black) board inside every electronic device
Copper traces etched onto a non-conducting substrate replace wired connections
Designed using EDA software (KiCad, Altium, Eagle) then manufactured in batches
Stands for Printed Circuit Board — multi-layer versions can stack dozens of copper layers
The standard system for transmitting and distributing electrical power commercially
Uses three AC voltages offset by 120° from each other
More efficient than single-phase — delivers more power with less conductor material
Industrial motors run on it because it produces a rotating magnetic field naturally
The grid you see on power lines is almost always this; your home splits off just one or two phases
